| Titular See of Cuncacestre   **Sancti Cuthberti**   Chester-le-Street | |

**Cuncacestre** ([Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street")[[1]](#cite_note-gcatholic-1)) was a seat of the [Anglo Saxon](/wiki/Anglo_Saxon "Anglo Saxon") [Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Bishop of Lindisfarne"), and subsists as a [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Chester-le-Street%2C_County_Durham.jpg/250px-St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Chester-le-Street%2C_County_Durham.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert%27s_Church,_Chester-le-Street,_County_Durham.jpg)

[Church of St Mary and St Cuthbert](/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert "Church of St Mary and St Cuthbert"), which is built on the site of the [Cathedral](/wiki/Cathedral "Cathedral") of Cuncacestre

## Start of the Diocese

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cuncacestre&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Start of the Diocese")]

The church was established to house the body of [Cuthbert of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Cuthbert_of_Lindisfarne "Cuthbert of Lindisfarne"), [Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Bishop of Lindisfarne") from 684 to 687. After his death he became one of the most venerated saints of the time, with a significant [cultus](/wiki/Cult_(religious_practice) "Cult (religious practice)") and the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Venerable_Bede "Venerable Bede") writing both a verse and prose biography of him. So when driven out of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") by [Viking](/wiki/Viking "Viking") raids in 875 the monks, led by [Eardulf of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Eardulf_of_Lindisfarne "Eardulf of Lindisfarne"), took [St Cuthbert's coffin](/wiki/St_Cuthbert%27s_coffin "St Cuthbert's coffin") along with other valuable items, including the [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels"). They wandered for seven years.

They eventually settled at [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street") (then called Cunecaster or Conceastre), at the site of the old Roman fort of [Concangis](/wiki/Concangis "Concangis"), in 883,[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000333,_336–337-2) on land granted to them by [Guthred](/wiki/Guthred "Guthred").[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELow_Low1881107-3)[[4]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESimeon,_Stevenson1855495,_664-4)

They built a wooden church and shrine for St Cuthbert's relics, dedicating it to St Mary and St Cuthbert. Though there was no shortage of stone in the ruins of Concangis they did not build a stone church; it has been suggested they did not intend to stay for as long as they eventually did. It was built within the Roman fort, which although abandoned over five hundred years before may have still offered some protection,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonner,_Standcliffe,_Rollason1989367–374-5) as well as access north and south along [Cade's Road](/wiki/Cade%27s_Road "Cade's Road") and to the sea by the [River Wear](/wiki/River_Wear "River Wear").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000337–40-6)

## Centre of Christianity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cuncacestre&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Centre of Christianity")]

Cuncacestre was the centre of Christianity for much of the northeast, because it was the seat of the [Bishop of Lindisfarne](/wiki/Bishop_of_Lindisfarne "Bishop of Lindisfarne"), making the church a cathedral.[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000337–40-6) The [diocese](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") stretched between the boundaries of [Danelaw](/wiki/Danelaw "Danelaw") at Teesside in the south, of [Alba](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Alba "Kingdom of Alba") at Lothian in the north and the [Irish Sea](/wiki/Irish_Sea "Irish Sea") in the west. The bishop's authority was confirmed by [Alfred the Great](/wiki/Alfred_the_Great "Alfred the Great"),[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000338-7) and for the next 112 years the community was based here, visited by kings [Athelstan](/wiki/Athelstan_of_England "Athelstan of England") and [Edmund](/wiki/Edmund_I_of_England "Edmund I of England") who both left gifts for the community, to add to the treasures brought from Lindisfarne.

Most notable among their treasures were the [Lindisfarne Gospels](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Gospels "Lindisfarne Gospels"), created in Lindisfarne around 715. They were bought with the monks after they left Lindisfarne. While here they were translated from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") into English, sometime between 947 and 968, by bishop [Aldred](/wiki/Aldred_of_Lindisfarne "Aldred of Lindisfarne") writing a [gloss](/wiki/Gloss_(annotation)#In_philology "Gloss (annotation)") in [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") above the text, making them the oldest surviving [English translation of the Gospels](/wiki/Old_English_Bible_translations "Old English Bible translations").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-BBC-10)

The Gospels and St Cuthbert's coffin were important relics for the diocese and the monks.

## Anglo Saxon Bishops

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cuncacestre&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Anglo Saxon Bishops")]

Further information: [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham")

## Transfer to Durham

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cuncacestre&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Transfer to Durham")]

Viking raids renewed under the reign of [Ethelred II](/wiki/Ethelred_II_of_England "Ethelred II of England"). In 995 Bishop Aldhun again found himself vulnerable to Danish attack and fled with St. Cuthbert's body to Ripon.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESelkirk2000338–340-11)

[Danegeld](/wiki/Danegeld "Danegeld") was paid again and peace was restored. Aldhun was on his way through [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England") to reestablish the see at Chester-le-Street when he received a divine vision that the body of St Cuthbert should remain in Durham. A stone chapel was built to receive the remains of St. Cuthbert's body and Aldhun began a great church on the site of [Durham Cathedral](/wiki/Durham_Cathedral "Durham Cathedral"), which was finished and consecrated in 999. The see and diocese of Lindisfarne (and Cuncacestre) was moved to Durham and the bishop's title became [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham#Saxon "Bishop of Durham"), with Aldhun becoming the first Bishop of Durham.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

The wooden church remained in place until replaced by a stone church in the mid 11th century,[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBonner,_Standcliffe,_Rollason1989367–374-5) and is now the [Church of St Mary and St Cuthbert](/wiki/Church_of_St_Mary_and_St_Cuthbert "Church of St Mary and St Cuthbert").

## Titular see

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Cuncacestre&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Titular see")]

Since 2020, the [titular see](/wiki/Titular_see "Titular see") of Cuncacestre has been held by David Evans, an auxiliary bishop in the [Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Birmingham](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Birmingham "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Birmingham").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The titular see, recreated in 1969, was previously held by Bishop [Robert Byrne](/wiki/Robert_Byrne_(bishop) "Robert Byrne (bishop)") (2014-2019), later [Bishop of Hexham and Newcastle](/wiki/Bishop_of_Hexham_and_Newcastle "Bishop of Hexham and Newcastle"), and Bishop [Alan Hopes](/wiki/Alan_Hopes "Alan Hopes") from 2003 until his appointment to the See of East Anglia in 2013,[[14]](#cite_note-CH.org-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Vatican_Information_Service-15) and before that had been held by [Hugh Lindsay](/wiki/Hugh_Lindsay_(bishop) "Hugh Lindsay (bishop)") (1969-1974), later [Bishop of Hexham and Newcastle](/wiki/Bishop_of_Hexham_and_Newcastle "Bishop of Hexham and Newcastle"), and [Owen Swindlehurst](/wiki/Owen_Swindlehurst "Owen Swindlehurst") (1977-1995).
